content.text: Building a REST API is the most common way to expose an agentic AI service to clients, other services, or orchestration layers. REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style — not a protocol — that uses standard HTTP semantics to create predictable, scalable interfaces for resources. Understanding REST principles from first principles will help you build agent APIs that are consistent, evolvable, and easy for clients to consume.

REST was defined by Roy Fielding in his 2000 doctoral dissertation as a set of architectural constraints that, when applied to a web API, produce properties like uniform interface, statelessness, cacheability, and layered architecture. A REST API is not just any HTTP API — it is specifically one that respects the semantics of HTTP methods, uses resources as the primary abstraction, communicates state through representations (usually JSON), and transfers state through hypermedia links.
In a REST API, every entity your system manages is a
. A resource has a stable URL (its

) that identifies it. Clients operate on resources using the five core HTTP methods: GET retrieves a representation of the resource, POST creates a new subordinate resource, PUT replaces a resource completely, PATCH updates it partially, and DELETE removes it. This uniform interface means any client that understands HTTP can understand your API without custom documentation for each operation.

For agentic AI systems, REST APIs serve three distinct roles: the agent calls third-party REST APIs as tools during its reasoning loop; the agent's capabilities are exposed to users via REST; and agent components (orchestrator, tool servers, memory service) communicate with each other via REST. Understanding REST from both the consumer and producer side is essential for building well-integrated agentic systems.

attrs.alt: REST API request lifecycle and HTTP methods reference

content.text: REST has become the dominant integration style for web services because it maps naturally to HTTP, which is universally accessible and supported by every programming language and deployment environment. Every LLM provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, Cohere), every vector database (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant), every cloud service (S3, DynamoDB, Azure OpenAI) exposes a REST API. Your agent needs to call these, and your services need to expose similar interfaces.

Beyond compatibility, REST's stateless constraint makes it ideal for agentic services. A stateless API does not store client session state between requests — all information required to process a request must be in the request itself. This makes REST services horizontally scalable (any server instance can handle any request), fault-tolerant (failed instances are simply replaced), and easy to reason about (no implicit state to track). For agent services that may run for seconds to minutes, stateless REST endpoints enable clean request isolation.

REST also provides a natural fit for agent tool interfaces. When an LLM agent calls a tool, it needs a clear, predictable interface. A REST endpoint with a well-defined request schema (validated with Pydantic), a clear HTTP method, and a documented status code convention is exactly what function-calling LLMs expect. Anthropic's tool use feature generates tool schemas that map directly onto REST API specifications.
every language, framework, and platform can make HTTP requests. No special SDK required for clients.
stateless requests can be handled by any server instance. Add or remove instances without draining sessions.
GET responses can be cached at every layer (browser, CDN, proxy). Dramatically reduces latency and load for read-heavy agent tool calls.
the same HTTP semantics work across all resources. Clients learn your API once and apply the same patterns everywhere.
load balancers, CDNs, and API gateways all understand HTTP and can operate between client and server transparently.
LLM tool use is expressed as function names and JSON parameters — REST maps these naturally to endpoints and request bodies.
Swagger UI, Postman, HTTPie, curl — every developer has REST tools. Auto-generated docs (FastAPI's /docs) are universally understandable.
REST requires one request per resource, making deeply nested or related resource fetching slow (N+1 problem). GraphQL or RPC solve this at the cost of added complexity.
REST endpoints return fixed schemas. Clients receive fields they do not need, or must make multiple calls to get all needed data.
REST's request/response model does not natively support streaming. Server-sent events or WebSockets must be layered on top for token streaming.
evolving REST APIs while maintaining backward compatibility requires careful versioning strategies (URI versioning, header versioning, or deprecation periods).
each REST call carries HTTP headers, TCP handshake overhead, and TLS overhead. For internal service-to-service calls at high frequency, gRPC is more efficient.
REST with JSON is weakly typed by default. OpenAPI spec generation (FastAPI) closes this gap but requires client code generation.
A REST API is built on four core concepts: resources, representations, methods, and status codes. Understanding how each maps to your application objects is the foundation of good API design.

A resource is any entity your system manages: an agent run, a tool invocation, a document, a user. Each resource has a URI that identifies it. URIs should be nouns (representing things), not verbs (representing actions). Good URI design is hierarchical and reflects the containment relationships between resources.

# Resource URI patterns
# Collection of agent runs
GET  /api/v1/agents/{agent_id}/runs
POST /api/v1/agents/{agent_id}/runs

# Individual agent run
GET    /api/v1/agents/{agent_id}/runs/{run_id}
DELETE /api/v1/agents/{agent_id}/runs/{run_id}

# Tool invocations within a run (sub-resources)
GET  /api/v1/agents/{agent_id}/runs/{run_id}/tool-calls
POST /api/v1/agents/{agent_id}/runs/{run_id}/tool-calls/{tool_call_id}/retry

# Avoid verb-based URIs (not REST):
POST /api/v1/executeAgent           # Wrong
POST /api/v1/agents/{id}/run-now   # Acceptable as action sub-resource

Each HTTP method carries specific semantic meaning. Violating these semantics breaks caching, idempotency, and client expectations. GET must be safe (no side effects) and idempotent (same result on repeat). DELETE and PUT must be idempotent. POST is neither safe nor idempotent.

from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Optional
import uuid

app = FastAPI()
store = {}  # in-memory stand-in for a database

class AgentRunCreate(BaseModel):
    query: str
    model: str = "claude-opus-4-5"
    max_tokens: int = 1024

class AgentRunUpdate(BaseModel):
    status: Optional[str] = None
    result: Optional[str] = None

@app.post("/api/v1/runs", status_code=201)          # POST: create, returns 201
async def create_run(body: AgentRunCreate):
    run_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
    store[run_id] = {"id": run_id, "status": "pending", **body.model_dump()}
    return store[run_id]  # return the created resource

@app.get("/api/v1/runs/{run_id}")                   # GET: read, safe + idempotent
async def get_run(run_id: str):
    if run_id not in store:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Run not found")
    return store[run_id]

@app.patch("/api/v1/runs/{run_id}")                  # PATCH: partial update
async def update_run(run_id: str, body: AgentRunUpdate):
    if run_id not in store:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Run not found")
    updates = body.model_dump(exclude_none=True)
    store[run_id].update(updates)
    return store[run_id]

@app.delete("/api/v1/runs/{run_id}", status_code=204) # DELETE: no body on success
async def delete_run(run_id: str):
    if run_id not in store:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=404, detail="Run not found")
    del store[run_id]

REST APIs use JSON as the dominant serialisation format. Request bodies carry the data needed to create or update a resource. Response bodies return the current representation of the affected resource. Always return the full updated resource in mutation responses — not just a status message — so clients can update their local state without a second GET.

# Request body: creating an agent tool
class ToolCreate(BaseModel):
    name: str                          # required
    description: str                   # required
    endpoint: str                      # required
    auth_type: str = "none"            # optional with default
    timeout_seconds: int = 30          # optional with default

# Response body: consistent envelope
class ToolResponse(BaseModel):
    id: str
    name: str
    description: str
    endpoint: str
    auth_type: str
    timeout_seconds: int
    created_at: str
    updated_at: str

# List response: pagination envelope
class ToolListResponse(BaseModel):
    items: list[ToolResponse]
    total: int
    page: int
    page_size: int
    has_next: bool

@app.get("/api/v1/tools", response_model=ToolListResponse)
async def list_tools(page: int = 1, page_size: int = 20):
    # Always use pagination for list endpoints
    start = (page - 1) * page_size
    items = list(tools_store.values())[start:start + page_size]
    return ToolListResponse(
        items=items,
        total=len(tools_store),
        page=page,
        page_size=page_size,
        has_next=(start + page_size) < len(tools_store),
    )

A well-designed REST API for an agentic system has several distinct layers: the HTTP layer (handled by the framework), the routing layer (dispatches to handlers), the validation layer (Pydantic models), the business logic layer (agent orchestration), the data access layer (repositories), and cross-cutting concerns (authentication, logging, error handling, rate limiting).

The routing layer defines URL patterns and associates them with handler functions. The validation layer ensures requests conform to expected schemas before reaching business logic. The business logic layer contains agent orchestration — starting runs, dispatching tools, polling for results. The data access layer abstracts persistence behind repository interfaces, making it easy to swap between in-memory storage (testing), PostgreSQL (production), and Redis (caching).

Cross-cutting concerns are best implemented as middleware. Authentication middleware checks tokens on every request. Logging middleware records request/response metadata. Rate limiting middleware enforces per-client request budgets. Error handling middleware catches unhandled exceptions and formats them as consistent JSON error responses.
You are building an agent API that will be consumed by browsers, mobile apps, or third-party integrations — REST's universal HTTP compatibility is essential here.
Your agent endpoints are coarse-grained (one request starts a full agent run, another polls its status) — REST's per-request overhead is amortised over the agent's work.
You need public-facing documentation that non-engineers can read — REST with OpenAPI/Swagger is the most widely understood API format.
You are building tool endpoints that LLM function-calling will invoke — REST maps naturally to the JSON schema function definitions LLMs expect.
Caching matters for your use case — REST's GET semantics enable transparent caching at CDN and proxy layers, reducing latency for expensive tool calls.

REST is less suitable when you need bidirectional real-time communication (use WebSockets), your agent components make thousands of small inter-service calls per second (use gRPC), or clients need flexible query structures to avoid over-fetching (consider GraphQL).

agent_api/
├── main.py              # FastAPI app factory, lifespan, includes routers
├── routers/
│   ├── agents.py        # /agents/* routes
│   ├── runs.py          # /runs/* routes
│   └── tools.py         # /tools/* routes
├── models/
│   ├── requests.py      # Pydantic request models
│   └── responses.py     # Pydantic response models
├── services/
│   ├── agent_service.py # Business logic
│   └── tool_service.py
├── repositories/
│   ├── run_repo.py      # Data access layer
│   └── tool_repo.py
├── middleware/
│   ├── auth.py
│   └── logging.py
└── config.py            # Settings from env vars

Here is a complete agentic REST API example that manages agent runs and supports polling for results. This pattern avoids the need for WebSockets by using the async polling pattern common in agent workflow APIs:
Anthropic's own Messages API is a REST API that follows these patterns precisely. The
endpoint accepts a JSON body with model, messages, and configuration, and returns a structured JSON response. Rate limiting is communicated via the
response headers. Errors always return structured JSON with a
Notice how the API is stateless: every request to
is self-contained — it carries the full conversation history in
. The server maintains no session. This means any Anthropic server instance can handle any request without sticky sessions, enabling massive horizontal scaling. The

endpoint returns 200 for success, 400 for invalid requests, 401 for bad API keys, 429 for rate limit exceeded, and 529 for service overload — a consistent, documented status code contract that clients can program against.

For your own agentic systems, model this pattern: make each endpoint self-contained, return consistent JSON schemas, communicate rate limits and errors through structured response fields, and version your API in the URI path (
) to allow evolution without breaking existing clients.
a common shortcut that breaks HTTP semantics and disables caching for reads. Use GET for reads, POST for creates, PATCH for partial updates.
are RPC-style, not REST. Use noun-based resource URIs:
in the body forces clients to parse the body to detect errors. Use 404 for not found, 422 for validation errors, 401 for auth failures.
works in development and causes memory exhaustion and timeouts in production. Always paginate list endpoints.
if success returns a resource object and errors return a bare string, clients must branch on every call. Define a consistent error envelope.
if a client retries a POST because the response was lost in transit, the server may create a duplicate resource. Support
returning raw database errors, stack traces, or internal service URLs in error responses reveals implementation details. Always sanitise error messages at the API boundary.
making breaking changes to a deployed API without versioning breaks all existing clients. Use URI versioning (
Design resources first — before writing code, identify the resources your system manages and their relationships. This prevents ad-hoc URI proliferation.
Use plural nouns for collection endpoints:
Return the full resource representation after creation or update — clients should not need a second GET.
Always include pagination for list endpoints. Start with cursor-based pagination (
) if the collection may be large or frequently updated.
Validate all inputs with Pydantic and return structured 422 responses. Never let raw validation errors reach the client.
Version your API from day one: prefix all routes with
Use consistent field naming: snake_case in JSON, ISO 8601 UTC for datetimes, UUID v4 for IDs.
) that Kubernetes probes can call without authentication.
Log request IDs on every request and include them in error responses so client error reports can be correlated with server logs.

REST APIs scale horizontally by adding stateless server instances behind a load balancer. Because no session state is stored on individual servers, the load balancer can route requests to any available instance without sticky sessions. This is REST's single greatest scalability advantage for agentic systems where individual runs may take 30-60 seconds.
Caching is REST's second performance lever. GET responses with appropriate

headers are cached by browsers, CDNs, and reverse proxies. For agent tool endpoints that read relatively stable data (tool definitions, user profiles, configuration), a 60-second TTL cache at the CDN layer can eliminate the vast majority of database reads.

For high-throughput agentic APIs, database connection pooling is the most common bottleneck. Use SQLAlchemy async with asyncpg and a pool size tuned to your database's max_connections limit. A typical starting point is

Rate limiting protects the API from both accidental overuse and intentional abuse. Implement rate limiting per API key at two levels: requests per second (burst limit) and tokens per minute (sustained throughput limit). For LLM-backed endpoints, the token budget matters more than request count because a single large request is far more expensive than many small ones.
Python's premier REST framework with automatic OpenAPI spec generation, Pydantic validation, async support, and Swagger UI at
. The default choice for new agent APIs.
the classic synchronous Python REST micro-framework. Mature ecosystem, wide documentation, best for simple or legacy agent endpoints.
async-first HTTP client for making REST API calls from within agent code. Supports connection pooling, timeouts, retries, and response streaming.
data validation library that powers FastAPI models. Validates, serialises, and deserialises JSON with Python type annotations and generates JSON Schema.
auto-generated interactive API documentation from OpenAPI specs. FastAPI generates these at
GUI clients for testing REST APIs during development. Both support environment variables for managing different deployment targets.
command-line REST client with a more readable syntax than curl, excellent for quick endpoint testing during development.
rate limiting libraries for FastAPI. Store counters in Redis for multi-instance deployments.
generates typed client SDKs in 40+ languages from an OpenAPI spec. Essential for providing client libraries to consumers of your agent API.
Chatty for complex queries, no native streaming
Public APIs, browser clients, LLM tool endpoints
Complex caching, no method semantics, steep learning curve
UI-driven APIs with complex, variable data shapes
Efficient binary protocol, bidirectional streaming, strong types via protobuf
No browser support without proxy, requires protobuf tooling
Internal microservice communication at high RPS
Stateful connections, complex load balancing, no HTTP caching
Real-time chat, live agent status, token streaming
Unidirectional streaming over standard HTTP, simple, proxy-friendly
One-way only, reconnection handling needed in clients
LLM token streaming layered onto REST endpoints
REST APIs for agentic systems handle sensitive data (user queries, LLM outputs, API keys) and must be secured at multiple layers:
use JWT bearer tokens (OAuth 2.0) or API keys. Never send credentials in URL query strings — they appear in server logs and browser history. Use the
all production REST APIs must use TLS. Configure your nginx reverse proxy to redirect HTTP to HTTPS and enable HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security).
validate all inputs before they reach business logic. Pydantic models in FastAPI provide automatic schema validation. Never pass raw request data directly to SQL queries or shell commands.
use parameterised queries or ORM abstractions. Never interpolate request data into SQL strings, even for seemingly harmless fields.
protect expensive agent endpoints from abuse. Apply different limits to different tiers (free vs. paid) and different endpoint costs (cheap read vs. expensive LLM call).
configure CORS carefully to allow only your own frontend origins. Never use the wildcard
set maximum request body sizes. Configure this at nginx (
) and at the framework level. A client sending a very large JSON body can exhaust server memory.
for destructive or expensive operations, support idempotency keys so retried requests do not create duplicate effects or charge users twice.
Think of a REST API as a filing cabinet for resources. Each drawer in the cabinet (a collection URL like
) holds folders of a particular type. Each folder (a resource URL like

) holds a document with the resource's current state. You GET a document to read it, POST a new document into a drawer to create it, PUT a replacement document in a folder, PATCH a sticky note of changes onto a folder, and DELETE a folder to remove it. The filing cabinet does not remember who you are between visits — every visit you must show your ID card (auth token) and tell it exactly what you want.

For async agent operations, think of the REST API as a ticket system. You POST a ticket describing your request. You receive a ticket ID and a 202 Accepted confirmation. You periodically GET the ticket to check its status. When the ticket shows 'completed', you GET the result. This polling pattern is RESTful, stateless, and easy to implement — and it is exactly how Anthropic's Batches API, OpenAI's Assistants API, and most long-running AI task APIs work.
REST is an architectural style — use HTTP methods semantically: GET reads, POST creates, PUT replaces, PATCH updates partially, DELETE removes.
Make every endpoint stateless: all information needed to process a request must be in the request itself.
Return consistent JSON envelopes for both successes and errors. Clients should not need conditional logic to parse response shapes.
Always paginate list endpoints, validate inputs with Pydantic, and return appropriate HTTP status codes.
For long-running agent operations, use the async polling pattern: return 202 Accepted with a polling URL, update status asynchronously, return result when complete.
Secure REST APIs with HTTPS, JWT bearer tokens, input validation, rate limiting, and CORS configuration.
Version your API from day one (
) and provide consistent error responses with enough context for clients to act on them.
